name: Transit Gateway Advanced Architectures
description: Designing multi-VPC and multi-region connectivity using Transit Gateway with route tables, peering, and Connect attachments
key_facts: TGW supports up to 5,000 attachments and 10,000 routes per route table
TGW peering enables inter-region connectivity without VPN overlays and supports static routes only
Multiple route tables enable network segmentation by associating attachments with specific domains (shared services, production, dev)
TGW Connect attachments support GRE tunneling for SD-WAN and third-party appliance integration with higher bandwidth than VPN
Appliance mode ensures symmetric routing for stateful inspection appliances attached to the TGW
common_misconceptions: TGW peering supports dynamic routing (it only supports static routes between peered TGWs)
TGW automatically routes between all attached VPCs (route table associations and propagations must be configured explicitly)

name: AWS Cloud WAN
description: Building and managing global wide-area networks with Cloud WAN core network policies and segment-based routing
key_facts: Cloud WAN uses a core network policy document (JSON) to define segments, attachment policies, and peering
Segments provide network isolation similar to TGW route table segmentation but managed as a global construct
Supports VPC, VPN, Direct Connect gateway, and TGW Connect attachments
Core network edges are deployed per-region and act as regional routers within the global mesh
Route sharing between segments requires explicit segment actions in the network policy
common_misconceptions: Cloud WAN replaces Transit Gateway (TGW remains the underlying construct; Cloud WAN orchestrates across regions and TGWs)
Cloud WAN segments automatically allow communication between each other (segments are isolated by default and require explicit sharing)

name: PrivateLink and VPC Endpoint Architectures
description: Designing private connectivity to AWS services and customer-hosted services using interface endpoints, gateway endpoints, and PrivateLink
key_facts: Interface endpoints create ENIs in the VPC subnets and support private DNS to override public service endpoints
Gateway endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB route traffic via route table entries without ENIs and are free of charge
PrivateLink allows exposing services to other VPCs or accounts via Network Load Balancer without VPC peering
Endpoint policies restrict which principals and resources can be accessed through the endpoint
Interface endpoints support security groups for fine-grained access control at the network level
common_misconceptions: Gateway endpoints work with all AWS services (only S3 and DynamoDB support gateway endpoints; other services use interface endpoints)
PrivateLink requires VPC peering between provider and consumer (PrivateLink is specifically designed to avoid peering)

name: Multi-VPC and Multi-Account Network Design
description: Designing scalable multi-VPC topologies including shared services VPCs, CIDR planning, and cross-account networking patterns
key_facts: VPC peering is non-transitive and best suited for a small number of VPC-to-VPC connections
Shared services VPC pattern centralizes DNS, endpoints, and NAT for cost optimization
Secondary CIDR blocks can be added to VPCs for address space expansion without re-architecting
AWS RAM shares subnets, TGWs, and other resources across accounts within an Organization
IP address planning must avoid overlapping CIDRs when peering or connecting to on-premises networks
common_misconceptions: VPC peering supports transitive routing (traffic cannot transit through a peered VPC to reach a third VPC)
Each VPC can only have one CIDR block (VPCs support multiple secondary CIDR blocks for expansion)

name: Hybrid DNS with Route 53 Resolver
description: Configuring bidirectional DNS resolution between AWS VPCs and on-premises networks using Route 53 Resolver endpoints
key_facts: Inbound endpoints allow on-premises DNS servers to resolve AWS private hosted zone records
Outbound endpoints allow VPC resources to forward DNS queries to on-premises DNS servers via Resolver rules
Resolver rules can be shared across accounts using AWS RAM for consistent DNS resolution
Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall filters outbound DNS queries to block malicious or unauthorized domains
Private hosted zones must be associated with VPCs where resolution is needed and support cross-account association
common_misconceptions: On-premises servers can resolve private hosted zones without Resolver inbound endpoints (the VPC DNS resolver at .2 is not accessible from on-premises without inbound endpoints)
Resolver rules automatically apply to all VPCs (rules must be associated with each VPC or shared via RAM)

name: Direct Connect with LAG and Resiliency
description: Implementing Direct Connect with Link Aggregation Groups, connection resiliency patterns, and virtual interface types
key_facts: LAG bundles up to 4 connections at the same speed and location using LACP for aggregate bandwidth
Private VIF connects to a VPC via VGW or Direct Connect Gateway; public VIF accesses AWS public services
Transit VIF connects to a Transit Gateway via Direct Connect Gateway for multi-VPC access
Maximum resiliency requires connections at two separate DX locations with redundant routers
Hosted connections are provisioned by partners at sub-1Gbps speeds; dedicated connections are 1, 10, or 100 Gbps
common_misconceptions: LAG connections can be different speeds (all connections in a LAG must have the same bandwidth)
A single Direct Connect location provides high availability (single location is a single point of failure; maximum resiliency requires two locations)

name: BGP Routing and Community Tags
description: Configuring BGP route advertisements, AS path prepending, and community tags for traffic engineering over Direct Connect and VPN
key_facts: BGP community tags control route scope: 7224:8100 limits to local region, 7224:8200 to continent, no tag is global
AS path prepending influences inbound traffic by making a path appear longer and less preferred
Local preference (MED) is used by AWS to influence outbound traffic from AWS to on-premises
Direct Connect BGP advertises up to 100 prefixes per session; exceeding this can cause session reset
Longest prefix match takes priority over AS path length in BGP route selection
common_misconceptions: AS path prepending controls outbound traffic from AWS (it influences inbound traffic to your network; AWS uses local preference for its outbound)
BGP community tags on public VIFs limit which regions can reach your network (communities control where AWS advertises your routes, not which regions can send traffic)

name: Site-to-Site VPN with Transit Gateway
description: Implementing scalable VPN architectures using Transit Gateway VPN attachments with ECMP, acceleration, and failover patterns
key_facts: TGW VPN attachments support ECMP across multiple VPN tunnels for aggregate throughput up to 50 Gbps
Each VPN connection provides two tunnels for redundancy across different AZs on the AWS side
Accelerated VPN uses AWS Global Accelerator to route traffic over the AWS backbone for improved performance
VPN as backup for Direct Connect provides automatic failover when configured with lower BGP preference
TGW supports multiple customer gateways for path diversity and increased throughput
common_misconceptions: A single VPN tunnel provides 1.25 Gbps per direction consistently (throughput varies and the 1.25 Gbps is a maximum; ECMP with multiple VPNs is needed for higher throughput)
VPN failover from Direct Connect is instant (BGP convergence time and health check intervals determine failover speed)

name: IPv6 and Dual-Stack Networking
description: Implementing IPv6 connectivity in VPCs with dual-stack configurations, egress-only internet gateways, and IPv6 CIDR planning
key_facts: VPCs support dual-stack with both IPv4 and IPv6 CIDR blocks; IPv6 CIDRs are /56 from Amazon or BYOIP
Egress-only internet gateway provides outbound-only IPv6 internet access similar to NAT Gateway for IPv4
Security groups support IPv6 rules; NACLs require separate rules for IPv6 traffic
IPv6 addresses in a VPC are globally unique and publicly routable unless restricted by security groups and NACLs
Not all AWS services support IPv6; ALB, NLB, and CloudFront support dual-stack but some older services do not
common_misconceptions: NAT Gateway supports IPv6 (NAT Gateway is IPv4 only; use egress-only internet gateway for IPv6 outbound access)
Enabling IPv6 on a VPC automatically enables it on all existing instances (existing instances must have IPv6 addresses assigned separately)

name: VPC Flow Logs Advanced Analysis
description: Configuring, querying, and analyzing VPC Flow Logs for network troubleshooting, security monitoring, and cost optimization
key_facts: Custom log format with version 5 fields includes vpc-id, subnet-id, instance-id, pkt-srcaddr, pkt-dstaddr, and traffic path
Flow logs can be queried with Athena using partition projection for cost-effective analysis at scale
Traffic path field identifies whether traffic routes through NAT gateway, TGW, VPC peering, or internet gateway
Logs do not capture DNS, DHCP, Amazon metadata, NTP, or Windows licensing traffic
Maximum aggregation interval is 10 minutes; 1-minute intervals provide more granular data at higher cost
common_misconceptions: Flow logs capture packet payloads (they capture metadata only including IPs, ports, protocol, and action)
Rejected traffic in flow logs always means a security group denied it (NACLs also reject traffic; the log shows the action but not which layer denied it)

name: VPC Reachability Analyzer and Network Manager
description: Using Reachability Analyzer for path analysis and Network Manager for global network visibility and monitoring
key_facts: Reachability Analyzer tests network path feasibility between two endpoints without sending actual packets
Identifies blocking components in the path such as security groups, NACLs, route tables, and missing peering
Network Manager provides a centralized dashboard for Transit Gateway, VPN, and Direct Connect health
Network Manager supports route analysis to identify overlapping or conflicting routes across TGW route tables
CloudWatch integration enables alerts on network topology changes and connectivity events
common_misconceptions: Reachability Analyzer sends actual traffic to test connectivity (it performs static configuration analysis without generating network traffic)
Network Manager manages VPC-level resources like subnets and ENIs (it focuses on global network infrastructure: TGW, VPN, DX, and Cloud WAN)

name: VPC Traffic Mirroring
description: Configuring traffic mirroring sessions to capture and inspect full packet data from ENIs for deep analysis and intrusion detection
key_facts: Mirrors inbound and outbound traffic from an ENI to a target ENI, NLB, or Gateway Load Balancer endpoint
Filter rules select which traffic to mirror based on protocol, port range, and direction to reduce data volume
Mirrored traffic uses VXLAN encapsulation and can be sent to IDS/IPS tools like Suricata or Zeek
Supported on Nitro-based instance types only; not all instance families support traffic mirroring
Mirror sessions have a maximum of 3 per ENI source and do not impact the performance of mirrored traffic
common_misconceptions: Traffic mirroring works on all EC2 instance types (only Nitro-based instances support traffic mirroring)
Traffic mirroring blocks malicious traffic inline (it is passive capture only; use Network Firewall for inline inspection)

name: AWS Network Firewall Rules and Deployment
description: Designing Network Firewall rule groups, deployment architectures, and inspection patterns for VPC traffic control
key_facts: Stateless rules evaluate each packet independently and process in priority order with pass, drop, or forward-to-stateful actions
Stateful rules use 5-tuple, domain list, or Suricata-compatible rule formats for connection-aware inspection
Centralized deployment model routes traffic through an inspection VPC using TGW for multi-VPC coverage
Distributed deployment places Network Firewall in each VPC for per-VPC inspection without TGW dependency
TLS inspection decrypts and re-encrypts HTTPS traffic using ACM certificates for encrypted traffic analysis
common_misconceptions: Network Firewall can be deployed without modifying VPC route tables (route tables must direct traffic to the firewall endpoint for inspection)
Stateful rules process before stateless rules (stateless rules evaluate first; matching traffic is then forwarded to stateful engine)

name: WAF Advanced Rules and Rate Limiting
description: Configuring AWS WAF web ACLs with rate-based rules, custom request inspection, and managed rule group strategies
key_facts: Rate-based rules block IPs exceeding a configurable threshold (100-20M) within a 5-minute evaluation window
Scope-down statements narrow rate-based rules to specific URI paths, headers, or query strings
Web ACL capacity units (WCU) limit rule complexity; maximum 5,000 WCU per web ACL
Managed rule groups from AWS and Marketplace provide pre-built protections for OWASP Top 10 and bot control
Custom response bodies return specific HTTP status codes and content when requests are blocked
common_misconceptions: WAF rate-based rules evaluate per-second (they aggregate requests over a 5-minute window before blocking)
WAF can protect any AWS resource (it only attaches to CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, AppSync, and Cognito user pools)

name: DDoS Mitigation with Shield Advanced and TLS Patterns
description: Implementing DDoS protection strategies with Shield Advanced, SRT engagement, and TLS termination patterns for secure connectivity
key_facts: Shield Advanced protects EC2, ELB, CloudFront, Global Accelerator, and Route 53 resources with cost protection and DRT access
Shield Response Team (SRT) can create WAF rules on your behalf during active DDoS attacks when proactive engagement is enabled
Health-based detection uses Route 53 health checks to improve DDoS detection sensitivity and reduce false positives
TLS termination at CloudFront or ALB offloads encryption processing and enables WAF inspection of decrypted traffic
End-to-end encryption patterns use ACM certificates on ALB with backend TLS to the origin for compliance requirements
common_misconceptions: Shield Standard provides DDoS Response Team access (only Shield Advanced includes SRT access and cost protection)
Shield Advanced automatically blocks all DDoS attacks without configuration (proactive engagement and health checks must be configured for optimal protection)
